coredata.dc:title: Rare-earth-free up and down-conversion dual-emission carbon dots for Cu2+ sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Hong, Yushuang
Nie, Zhengpei
Tian, Xuelian
Sun, Jingfang
Zhou, Qiuju
Liang, Wenbin
Chen, Shihong
Huang, Jin
Tan, Kejun
Dong, Lin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, up- and down-conversion dual-emission CDs without rare-earth (UD D-CDs) were synthesized using RhB and 1,4-Diaminoanthraquinone as precursors. The synthesized UD D-CDs exhibited dual emissions at 496 and 580 nm under 260 and 865 nm excitation, respectively. The fluorescence emission mechanism, including contributions from carbon nuclei, surface states, molecular states, and internal defect states, was discussed through the separation and purification of UD D-CDs. Based on the interaction between UD D-CDs and copper ions (Cu2+), a dual-mode ratio fluorescence probe was developed to detect and quantify Cu2+. The up-conversion ratio fluorescent probe shows a linear range of 0.0500–15.0 μM, with a detection limit as low as 2.76 nM. This method has been successfully applied to detecting Cu2+ in human serum and has potential applications in biochemical analysis and biological imaging. The successful preparation of up-conversion fluorescent carbon dots without rare earth elements and the ability to perform low-damage detection in high-background biological samples provide a new approach to constructing non-rare earth up-conversion probes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission
Carbon dots
Ratio Fluorescence
Up-conversion
Cu2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset: HR TEM and size distribution histogram), (b) XRD image, (c) FTIR, (d) UV–vis absorption and excitation spectra (inset: The image of UD D-CDs under sunlight and 254 nm excitation light) of UD D-CDs. (e) The FL emission spectra of UD D-CDs at different excitation wavelengths, (f) Three-dimensional FL map of UD D-CDs. Note: the emission peak at 430 nm in the up-conversion spectrum is the scattering peak.

The FL spectra of UD D-CDs with various concentrations of Cu2+ at (a) λ ex = 260 nm and (d) λ ex = 865 nm, (c) the image under λ ex = 254 nm, the relationship between F x/F 0 and the concentrations of Cu2+ at (b) λ ex = 260 nm and (e) λ ex = 865 nm, inset: Linear fitting curve. Note: the emission peak at 430 nm in the up-conversion spectrum is the scattering peak.

(a) FL emission spectra of RhB with and without Cu2+, (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of UD D-CDs with or without Cu2+, (c) FL decay curves of UD D-CDs with or without Cu2+, High-resolution narrow scan XPS spectra of (d) Cu 2p, (e) XAES spectrum of the UD D-CDs with Cu2+, cyclic voltammograms of (f) UD D-CDs and (g) Cu2+, (h) The diagram of the basic principle of UD D-CDs FL and the quenching mechanism of Cu2+.
The luminescence mechanism of UD D-CDs.
Analysis parameters of down- and up-conversion ratio fluorescence assay.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: a) F X and F 0 represent the ratio of the 496 nm FL intensity to the 580 nm FL intensity of the UD D-CDs with and without Cu2+, respectively.

head.ce:title: Rare-earth-free up and down-conversion dual-emission carbon dots for Cu2+ sensing

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, up- and down-conversion dual-emission CDs without rare-earth (UD D-CDs) were synthesized using RhB and 1,4-Diaminoanthraquinone as precursors. The synthesized UD D-CDs exhibited dual emissions at 496 and 580 nm under 260 and 865 nm excitation, respectively. The fluorescence emission mechanism, including contributions from carbon nuclei, surface states, molecular states, and internal defect states, was discussed through the separation and purification of UD D-CDs. Based on the interaction between UD D-CDs and copper ions (Cu2+), a dual-mode ratio fluorescence probe was developed to detect and quantify Cu2+. The up-conversion ratio fluorescent probe shows a linear range of 0.0500–15.0 μM, with a detection limit as low as 2.76 nM. This method has been successfully applied to detecting Cu2+ in human serum and has potential applications in biochemical analysis and biological imaging. The successful preparation of up-conversion fluorescent carbon dots without rare earth elements and the ability to perform low-damage detection in high-background biological samples provide a new approach to constructing non-rare earth up-conversion probes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new type of luminescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention because of their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and excellent optical properties[1]. At present, CDs have been widely used in biological imaging[2,3], information encryption[4], biochemical analysis[5,6], light-emitting diode (LED)[7,8], etc. However, most reported CDs exhibit single-wavelength emission, which limits their wide application due to their single tone, poor visualization effect, and lack of self-calibration ability[9]. In contrast, multi-wavelength emission CDs can avoid these defects[10]. Zhao's group synthesized dual-emission CDs with fluorescence (FL) at 445 nm and 617 nm using urea and citric acid as a precursor, achieving ratio FL detection of tetracycline with a good visualization effect [11]. Multi-emission CDs solve the application limitation of single-emission CDs, offer improved visualization effects, and enhance sensing sensitivity to a certain extent.

Sensing analysis is an important application of CDs, used for both ion and molecular sensing[12], including in vitro quantitative detection and in vivo real-time monitoring. Most CDs exhibit down-conversion FL when excited by ultraviolet light[13,14]. However, using high-energy ultraviolet light can cause damage to biological organisms and cannot avoid background interference, thus limiting their applications in organisms. Up-conversion FL can overcome these shortcomings[15]. However, some up-conversion multi-emission CDs are either doped with rare earth elements or require complex, multi-step synthesis processes[16–19]. Rare-earth elements are often used to prepare up-conversion nanomaterials due to their long-lived intermediate energy levels and 4f-4f transitions in their configuration[20]. While the research on rare-earth-based up-conversion emission CDs is well-developed, the study of rare-earth-free up-conversion dual-emission CDs is still in its early stages. Therefore, the preparation of rare-earth-free up-conversion dual-emission CDs is of great significance.

Copper is an essential trace element in the human body[21]. An up-conversion FL probe can effectively avoid the interference of background FL in the determination of Cu2+ in vivo. In this work, dual-emission CDs (UD D-CDs) with up-/down-conversion were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method with Rhodamine B (RhB) and 1,4-diaminoanthraquinone as precursors. Under excitation at 260 nm and 865 nm, the CDs emit fluorescence at 496 nm and 580 nm, respectively. UD D-CDs were separated by column chromatography to analyze their FL mechanism, including contributions from the carbon core, surface states, defect states, and molecular states. In addition, based on the unique fluorescence characteristics of the UD D-CDs, a dual-mode (up-/down-conversion FL) ratio probe was constructed for the ultra-sensitive determination of Cu2+ in blood. Experimental results demonstrate that the prepared UD D-CDs can be used for nondestructive testing in vivo.

Rhodamine B (RhB), 1,4-Diaminoanthraquinone, ethylene glycol and copper dihydrate chloride (CuCl2·2H2O) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium chloride (NaCl) was purchased from Kelon Chemical reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). All chemicals were analytical reagents and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 Ω.cm at 25 °C) was used throughout the experiments. A disodium hydrogen phosphate-sodium hydroxide (Na2HPO4-NaOH) buffer solution was used to control the acidity.

UD D-CDs' FL spectra were recorded with an FL spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) with a slit width of 10 nm for excitation and emission at λ
ex = 260 nm and of 20 nm for excitation and emission at λ
ex = 865 nm, respectively. The absorption spectra were measured with a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometry (Tokyo, Japan). The pH values were measured by the FE20/EL20 pH meter (Shanghai, China). FL lifetime was measured by Fls980 full function steady state/transient FL spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

The target CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using RhB and 1,4-Diaminoanthraquinone as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The steps are as follows: 1,4-Diaminoanthraquinone (100 mg) was mixed with RhB (20 mg) and dissolved in ethylene glycol (4 mL) and ultrapure water (1 mL). Then the mixture was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene liner (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 8 h in a stainless-steel autoclave. The product was cooled to room temperature and dissolved in ethanol. After centrifugation at 12000 rpm/min for 10 min, the supernatant was dialyzed using a cellulose ester dialysis membrane (500 MWCO). The resulting solution is refrigerated at 4 °C for standby.

The detection of Cu2+ was carried out in Na2HPO4-NaOH buffer (pH=11.16) at room temperature. Specifically, 100 μL UD D-CDs and 100 μL Na2HPO4-NaOH buffer solutions were added into a 2 mL Eppendorf (EP) tube. Different concentrations of Cu2+ were added to the above mixture and diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water, followed by vortex oscillation. After a certain time, the FL spectra of up-/down-conversion were measured.

The target CDs were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method with RhB and 1,4-diaminoanthraquinone as precursors. The optimization of synthesis conditions was discussed in detail in section S1 of the supporting information (SI). The results are shown in Figures S1-S2. The morphology and crystal lattice of UD D-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, UD D-CDs are uniformly distributed small particles with an average diameter of about 1.8 nm. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) reveals that the lattice spacing of UD D-CDs is 0.18 nm, corresponding to the (101) plane of graphene [22]. XRD in Fig. 1b shows a wide peak centered at 29.5° and 40°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) plane of graphene[23].

The surface functional groups of UD D-CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1c, several characteristic absorption peaks, including 1662 cm−1, 1609 cm−1, and 677 cm−1, correspond to ν(C=O), ν(C=C), and γ(=C–H)[24], respectively, which indicates that polyaromatic structure is formed in UD D-CDs during carbonization. In addition, there is a broad vibration absorption peak at 3600 cm−1-2960 cm−1, corresponding to ν (O–H) and ν (N–H)[25,26], respectively. Furthermore, as shown in the UV–vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 1d, there is a noticeable peak (black line) at 250 nm, attributed to the π-π* transition of the C=C bond[11]. The absorption peak at 550 nm in the visible region can be attributed to the UD D-CDs' surface functional group defects[9]. The types and relative contents of surface functional groups of UD D-CDs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), as shown in Figure S3. The XPS data reveal the presence of various electron-withdrawing groups on the surface of UD D-CDs, such as carboxyl and amino groups. This result is consistent with the zeta potential (Figure S4) and the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1c). These groups provide the basis for the luminescence of UD D-CDs.

To study the FL properties of UD D-CDs in detail, we analyzed the FL emission spectra (Fig. 1d, 1e, 1f). Fig. 1d shows two prominent emission peaks at 496 nm and 580 nm (red line). The UD D-CDs solution displays a light cyan color under 254 nm excitation (inset of Fig. 1d). The FL QYs of these two emission peaks are 11.99 % and 49.01 % relative to sodium fluorescein and RhB, respectively (Figure S5). Fig. 1e shows that UD D-CDs exhibit excitation independence in the range of 240–280 nm. Surprisingly, UD D-CDs not only show down-conversion dual-emission but also exhibit excellent up-conversion dual-emission. Fig. 1f shows that as the excitation wavelength increases from 840 nm to 880 nm, the intensity of the up-conversion FL emission peak, at the same position as the down-conversion peak, gradually increases and reaches its maximum at 865 nm excitation. This dual-emission behavior of up-conversion is infrequent.

To analyze the FL source of UD D-CDs, we carried out a series of characterizations. Firstly, the excitation spectrum of the UD D-CDs was measured (Fig. 1d, green line). It can be observed that the maximum excitation peak at 496 nm is consistent with the corresponding exciton absorption peak, indicating that the band gap state of UD D-CDs is the primary source of emission at 496 nm[22]. In addition, CDs-1 and CDs-2 were synthesized under the same conditions with RhB and 1,4-Diaminoanthraquinone as precursors, respectively. The emission spectrum of the CDs-1 at 260 nm excitation wavelength was detected, as shown in Figure S6. The result reveals that the CDs-1 has a strong FL at 580 nm. So, we speculate that the emission at 580 nm is inherited from the molecular FL of the precursor RhB.

To further explore multiple emission sources, UD D-CDs were gradient eluted by column chromatography for separation and purification. Ethyl acetate and petroleum ether in a 2:1 ratio were used as the initial eluent, followed by dichloromethane, and finally ethanol (Fig. 2 ). After purification by silica gel column chromatography, CDs with different color FL were obtained, as shown in Figure S7. R-CDs, O-CDs, Y-CDs, G-CDs, and C-CDs were selected for subsequent analysis.

The 3D spectra of the five CDs in Figure S8 show that there are multiple emission centers located at 440 nm, 500 nm, 520 nm, 580 nm, and 620 nm. FL emission spectra in Figure S9 display that the emission at 440 nm, 500 nm, 580 nm, and 620 nm are excitation independent. In contrast, the emission at 520 nm is excitation-dependent due to the uneven distribution of CDs on the surface[27]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Figure S10) shows that the broad absorption in the 250 nm and low-energy region (400–700 nm) further increases the possibility of multi-emission from UD D-CDs.

Raman spectra of G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs, and R-CDs were also measured and shown in Figure S11. The results show two peaks at 1300–1400 cm−1 and 1500–1600 cm−1, corresponding to the graphite carbon of sp3 (D band) and sp2 (G band)[28], respectively. The I D/I G ratios were calculated as 4.16, 1.95, 1.23 and 1.99, respectively. The irregular change of I D/I G with emission shift indicates that the graphitization degree is not why the FL emission of UD D-CDs changes from blue to red[29]. At the same time, the surface functional groups were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy (Figure S12). The FL properties of each CD in various solvents and at different pH levels were measured (Figures S13 and S14). The results show that the FL remains stable only at 420 nm. These findings indicate that the excitation of the 420 nm emission is independent and exhibits good stability, with its FL emission unaffected by external factors such as pH and solvent. This is consistent with the characteristics of carbon nuclear luminescence[30]. Therefore, the blue emission at 420 nm is classified as carbon nuclear luminescence related to sp2 carbon.

XPS was used to analyze the surface functional groups of G-CDS, Y-CDS, O-CDs and R-CDs, and the results are shown in Figure S15. According to the investigation, the content of the C=O bond in the O1s spectrum reflects the surface oxidation degree of CDs. The surface defects caused by surface oxidation can be used as the capturing center of excitons to produce FL related to the surface state [31]. The XPS results of the three CDs are shown in Table S2. The content of C=O increases with the color of FL changing from green to red. The existence of the C=O bond leads to the electron-rich properties of the inner surface of CDs. Abundant electrons can occupy the energy level of the surface state and enhance the Fermi level of CDs, resulting in FL[31]. With the increasing content of the C=O bond, the band gap between the conduction band and valence band of CDs decreases, resulting in the redshift of FL[32]. In addition, the investigation shows that sp3 carbon will lead to internal defects on CDs[33]. When irradiated by ultraviolet light, the corresponding emission traps are activated and emit specific colors when illuminated by specific excitation wavelengths[32], which generally shows the excitation dependence of FL. This corresponds to the excitation dependence of the previous Y-CDs. In conclusion, the green, yellow, and red emission of UD D-CDs are attributed to the surface state luminescence caused by C=O and intrinsic defect luminescence.

The orange emission at 580 nm can be attributed to molecular luminescence. In general, molecular state luminescence has high QY and excitation independence and susceptibility [34]. To verify this conclusion, we measured the relative QY of O-CDs in water and ethanol as 43.50 % and 80.76 %, respectively (Figure S16). Figures S9a, S13b and S14b show that O-CDs are excitation-independent and susceptible to external conditions such as solvent and pH. So, 580 nm orange emission does come from the molecular luminescence of the precursor RhB.
Based on the above, the FL emission mechanism of UD D-CDs is mainly carbon nuclear, surface, defect, and molecular states (Scheme 1 ).

Observing the up-conversion emissions 3D diagram of the separated components (Figure S17) shows that each separated component contains weak up-conversion FL, and its luminescence position corresponds to the up-conversion FL of UD D-CDs. The mechanism of up-conversion FL still needs to be explored. This excellent up-conversion FL may be due to the continuous energy transfer caused by multi-photon absorption. To further investigate the mechanism of up-conversion FL, the emission spectra of the CDs-1 and CDs-2 at 865 nm were measured, as shown in Figure S6. The results show no up-conversion FL for CDs-1 and CDs-2, which excludes the possibility that the up-conversion FL comes from the precursor. The absorption of two or more photons simultaneously may lead to light emission shorter than the excitation wavelength[35,36]. In this experiment, infrared light in the range of 840–880 nm is used as the excitation light source, which causes the multi-photon absorption, resulting in the anti-Stokes shift of 350–250 nm and FL emission at 496 nm and 580 nm. Therefore, the up-conversion FL process may be that the ground state electrons absorb energy and transfer it to the excited state. Then some excited state electrons transfer their energy to other excited state electrons, further transferring to a higher excited state. When the electrons in the higher energy state return to the ground state, a significant amount of energy is released, resulting in up-conversion FL.

Based on the excellent FL properties of UD D-CDs, it is possible to develop visual sensing using dual-emission of 496 nm and 580 nm. Therefore, we investigated the stability of UD D-CDs in section S2 of SI. Figures S18 and 19 show that the UD D-CDs have good photobleaching, pH stability and salt resistance.

To demonstrate potential analytical applications, the UD D-CDs were used as FL ratio probes for Cu2+ detection. The effects of different conditions on the determination of Cu2 + were discussed in section S3 of the SI and shown in Figure S20. Under the optimum conditions, the UD D-CDs interact with Cu2+, resulting in quenching of 496 nm down- and up-conversion FL while keeping 580 nm down-/up-conversion FL almost unchanged (Fig. 3 ). Based on this, a sensitive dual-readout (down-/up-conversion FL) ratio FL assay of Cu2+ was developed.

The analysis parameters were obtained under the optimal conditions, as shown in Table 1 . The limit of detection (LOD) is 5.00 nM for the down-conversion ratio FL assay and 2.76 nM for up-conversion ratio FL assay, which is lower than that of some other reported methods (Table S3). Compared to other fluorescent probes, this ratio FL probe offers better visualization. Under the irradiation of a 254 nm UV lamp, the color of UD D-CDs changes from cyan, yellow, to orange (Fig. 3c), which is more conducive to directly identifying the concentration change of analytes. The developed up-conversion ratio FL probe is of great significance for in vivo detection. It can avoid the interference of background FL and reduce the damage to organisms because of its low excitation energy.

The response of FL emission from precursor RhB at 580 nm to Cu2+ was measured, as shown in Fig. 4 a. The results showed that the FL of RhB was not affected by the addition of Cu2+. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of FL at 496 nm was mainly discussed later. UV–vis absorption spectra and FL lifetime were measured to explore the response mechanism of the probe to Cu2+ (Fig. 4b and c). The results show no absorption in the 400 nm-650 nm band of Cu2+ absorption spectra, indicating that the FL quenching is not caused by the internal filtering effect or resonance energy transfer[37]. The addition of Cu2+ does not affect the position of the probe absorption peak but reduces the intensity of the absorption peak. The main reason is that cu2+ destroys the surface functional groups of UD D-CDs and reduces their conjugation degree, resulting in a decrease in absorption intensity. In addition, the FL lifetimes of the probe at 496 nm without and with Cu2+ are 3.51 ns and 3.46 ns, respectively. Therefore, the FL quenching behavior may be a combination of dynamic and static quenching.

To further investigate the mechanism of FL quenching, the Cu2+ probe was analyzed by XPS, XAES and cyclic voltammetry. After adding Cu2+, new convolution peaks appeared at 406 eV and 536.2 eV in the high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s and O1s in Figure S21, corresponding to N-Cu[38] and O-Cu bonds, respectively. This confirmed that Cu2+ successfully complexed with surface functional groups. In the high-resolution XPS spectra of Cu 2p (Fig. 4d), the spin–orbit splittings of Cu 2p 3/2 and 2p 1/2 were observed at 931.16 eV and 951.23 eV, indicating the presence of Cu0 in the system[23]. The peaks at 932.65 eV and 953.80 eV also show the presence of unreduced Cu2+, confirming the complexation of Cu2+ with surface groups. In addition, the XAES spectra (Fig. 4e) show a peak centered at 573 eV, which confirms the existence of Cu2+ [39]. At the same time, the convolution peak at 563.6 eV further confirms the generation of Cu0 [40]. Therefore, a redox reaction may occur in the system after adding Cu2+.

To verify this conjecture, we scanned the cyclic voltammograms of UD D-CDs with and without Cu2+ under anaerobic conditions. After adding Cu2+, new reduction and oxidation peaks of UD D-CDs appeared at about 0.0550 V and 0.410 V (Figure S22). The reduction peak at −0.580 V for UD D-CDs was also enhanced, further supporting this hypothesis. The cyclic voltammetry of UD D-CDs and Cu2+ was analyzed in detail (Fig. 4f-4 g). According to formula (1), the E red of UD D-CDs and Cu2+ are −0.376 V and −0.336 V, respectively. E LUMO are −4.02 V and −4.06 V. In addition, formula (2) was used to estimate the E HOMO of UD D-CDs and Cu2+, where E g is the HOMO-LUMO energy gap[41]. We can roughly calculate the Eg of UD D-CDs from the edge of the absorption spectrum as 2.09 V. In addition, the Eg of Cu2+ is 1.14 V[40]. Therefore, the EHOMO values of UD D-CDs and Cu2+ were calculated as −6.114 V and −5.20 V, respectively.
(1)
E
LUMO
= -
E
red
+ 4.40
(2)
E g
=
E
LUMO
-
E
HOMO

According to the energy levels of HOMO and LUMO, there may be a non-radioactive photo-induced electron transfer between UD D-CDs and Cu2+ [40]. On the one hand, the ground state electrons of UD D-CDs gain energy in the HOMO orbit and then jump to the LUMO orbit. When the excited state electrons fall to HOMO, some are transferred to LUMO of Cu2+, limiting the radioactive transition of UD D-CDs, resulting in FL quenching. On the other hand, strong FL is produced due to the recombination of electrons and holes. When Cu2+ is added to UD D-CDs, Cu2+ is reduced to copper on the surface of UD D-CDs. Copper may load some excited electrons of UD D-CDs, interrupting the recombination of electrons and holes, and reducing the FL of UD D-CDs (Fig. 4h).

The practicability of the probe is closely related to its selectivity and anti-interference ability. Both section S4 in SI and Figure S23 show that UD D-CDs possess specific recognition ability for Cu2+, enabling the determination and visual resolution of copper ions. In addition, compared to other probes, this is the first time an up-conversion dual-emission ratio fluorescent probe has been used for the sensitive detection of Cu2+ . As shown in Table S3, the proposed UD D-CDs probe demonstrates a lower LOD than some other reported FL probes. The applications of UD D-CDs in human serum samples are detailed in section S5 of SI. The results (Table S4 and S5) show that the recovery of the up-conversion ratio FL assay is better than that of the down-conversion ratio FL assay, indicating that the former effectively eliminates the interference of bioluminescence. This highlights the advantages of the up-conversion ratio FL assay and demonstrates the reliability of UD D-CDs for practical applications.

In this work, UD D-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method for the first time. It provides a reference for the synthesis of rare-earth-free multi-emission up-conversion nanomaterials. FL mechanism study shows that the FL emission mechanism includes carbon nucleus, molecular, surface, and internal defect states, offering a valuable reference for other researchers studying multi-emission CDs FL. The up-conversion dual-emission ratio FL assay based on UD D-CDs has been successfully applied to detect Cu2+ in human serum, which provides an idea for exploring the practical application of dual-emission up-conversion in low damage and high sensitivity detection in organisms. Although rare-earth free UD D-CDs were synthesized using a simple method in this work, the emission peaks were relatively close together, covering only the 500 to 600 nm range, which limited the visualization effect. Therefore, further exploration is needed to develop methods for synthesizing rare-earth free UD D-CDs with larger dual-peak spacing and wider spectral coverage. Additionally, the established fluorescent probe has not yet been applied to cell imaging or in vivo detection, indicating that its potential for biological applications requires further enhancement.

Yushuang Hong: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Zhengpei Nie: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xuelian Tian: Validation, Data curation. Jingfang Sun: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Qiuju Zhou: Writing – review & editing. Wenbin Liang: Supervision, Project administration. Shihong Chen: Supervision, Project administration. Jin Huang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Kejun Tan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Lin Dong: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the financial support from the Project for Chongqing University Innovation Research Group of Chongqing Education Committee (CXQT19008), and the Chongqing Talent Plan for Innovation and Entrepreneurship Demonstration Team (CQYC201903243).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124920.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of carbon dots with strong luminescence in both dispersed and aggregated states by tailoring sulfur doping

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, M. Zheng, H. Wang, Y. Meng, D. Wang, L. Liu, Q. Zeng, X. Xu, D. Zhou, H. Sun, Synthesis of carbon dots with strong luminescence in both dispersed and aggregated states by tailoring sulfur doping, J Colloid Interface Sci 609 (2021) 54-64. DOI: 10.1016/j.jcis.2021.11.179.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategy to synthesize tunable multiemission carbon dots and their multicolor visualization application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.P. Zhu, J.Y. Li, L.X. Gao, J. Xiong, K.J. Tan, Strategy to Synthesize Tunable Multiemission Carbon Dots and Their Multicolor Visualization Application, ACS Appl Mater Interfaces 13(28) (2021) 33354-33362. DOI: 10.1021/acsami.1c07260.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cu, Zn dopants boost electron transfer of carbon dots for antioxidation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Xue, T. Zhang, X. Wang, Q. Zhang, S. Huang, L. Zhang, L. Zhang, W. Zhu, Y. Wang, M. Wu, Q. Zhao, P. Li, W. Wu, Cu,Zn Dopants Boost Electron Transfer of Carbon Dots for Antioxidation, Small 17(31) (2021) 2102178. DOI: 10.1002/smll.202102178.

Zhang, Fluorescent carbon dots embedded in mesoporous silica nanospheres: A simple platform for Cr(VI) detection in environmental water, J Hazard Mater 415 (2021) 125699. DOI: 10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.125699.
